title: AI Data Center Optical Interconnect Buildout
catalyst: NVIDIA's next-generation GPU platforms (Blackwell Ultra, Rubin) require dramatically more bandwidth between chips, driving massive investment into optical interconnect technology. NVIDIA has publicly committed to co-packaged optics and active electrical cables, creating a multi-billion dollar TAM for suppliers of high-speed connectivity components.
thesis: As AI clusters scale from thousands to millions of GPUs, the interconnect bottleneck becomes the binding constraint on performance. Electrical cables can't keep up beyond 800G — optical solutions are the only path to 1.6T and 3.2T speeds needed for next-gen training runs. Companies designing the silicon and optics for these links are in the critical path of every hyperscaler's capex plan.

ticker_rationale.CRDO: Credo Technology is the leading supplier of Active Electrical Cables (AECs) for AI data centers, with 201% YoY revenue growth driven by hyperscaler demand. Their SerDes IP and AEC technology sit directly in NVIDIA's supply chain for rack-scale AI interconnects.
ticker_rationale.MRVL: Marvell Technology's custom silicon business (doubling to $1.5B in FY2026) designs the connectivity chips that go inside AI networking equipment. Their PAM4 DSPs and custom ASIC platform serve every major hyperscaler building AI infrastructure.

risks: NVIDIA could vertically integrate interconnect technology, reducing reliance on third-party suppliers
AI capex spending could decelerate if training efficiency gains reduce hardware demand
Competitive pressure from Broadcom's custom silicon business could compress margins
summary: NVIDIA's next-gen GPU platforms require 1.6T+ optical interconnects, creating a multi-billion-dollar TAM for companies like Credo and Marvell that design the connectivity silicon and cables sitting in the critical path of every hyperscaler's AI infrastructure buildout.
body_md:
## Setup

The AI data center buildout has created an interconnect bottleneck that is rapidly becoming the binding constraint on cluster performance. As training runs scale from thousands to hundreds of thousands of GPUs, the bandwidth between chips — not the chips themselves — determines system throughput. Electrical interconnects hit physical limits beyond 800G, forcing the industry toward optical solutions for 1.6T and 3.2T speeds [1]. At GTC 2026, NVIDIA confirmed that its NVL576 rack ships with co-packaged optics (CPO) integrated into the NVSwitch, replacing pluggable transceivers and signaling that optical interconnects are no longer optional — they are the default architecture for next-generation AI clusters [1][2].

The total addressable market for AI optical interconnects is inflecting sharply. Hyperscalers collectively committed over $630 billion in AI infrastructure capex for 2026, and each new GPU rack requires proportional spending on high-speed cables, optical DSPs, and networking silicon [3]. Companies positioned in this critical path — designing the SerDes IP, active electrical cables, PAM4 DSPs, and custom networking ASICs — are capturing an outsized share of this spend.

## Why Now

Three factors make the timing urgent. First, the 800G-to-1.6T networking transition is beginning in 2026-2027, requiring next-generation connectivity products across the entire data center fabric [2]. Credo has announced a $500M+ optical revenue target by FY2028 and acquired DustPhotonics in April 2026 to build a vertically integrated connectivity stack spanning SerDes, DSP, silicon photonics, and system integration [2]. Second, NVIDIA's Rubin platform — the successor to Blackwell — demands even higher bandwidth between compute and memory, pulling forward optical adoption timelines. Jensen Huang confirmed at GTC 2026 that co-packaged optics will arrive with Feynman NVLink 8 CPO switches in 2028, creating a multi-year design-win runway for connectivity suppliers [1]. Third, every major hyperscaler is simultaneously building million-GPU clusters, creating parallel demand streams that exceed the supply capacity of the interconnect ecosystem.

## Company-Specific Edge

Credo Technology (CRDO) pioneered the active electrical cable (AEC) category and has become the leading supplier for AI data center interconnects. Q3 FY2026 revenue of $407M grew 201.5% YoY and 52% sequentially, with GAAP net income of $157M and non-GAAP gross margins of 68.6% [4]. The company's HiWire AECs, optical PAM4 DSPs, and SerDes chiplets are deployed across multiple hyperscaler platforms. CEO Bill Brennan's strategic pivot from pure SerDes IP licensing to system-level hardware created a new product category that now generates over $1.3B in annualized revenue [4]. At 21.7x forward P/E with 201% revenue growth and 31.8% profit margins, CRDO offers a rare combination of hypergrowth and profitability.

Marvell Technology (MRVL) brings complementary scale and custom silicon expertise. The company's custom silicon business doubled to $1.5B in FY2026, with engagements across all four major hyperscalers and 18 multigenerational XPU/XPU attach sockets [5]. FY2026 revenue of $8.19B grew 42% YoY, and management targets $15B revenue by FY2028 — implying ~83% growth over two years [5][6]. Marvell's PAM4 and coherent DSPs (via the Inphi acquisition) are the industry standard for 800G/1.6T optical interconnects, and the NVIDIA alliance positions Marvell as a co-development partner for next-generation networking solutions. At 20.3x forward P/E with 32.6% profit margins and D/E of just 33.5%, MRVL offers growth at a reasonable valuation.

## Numbers That Matter

**Credo (CRDO)**:
- Revenue: $407M Q3 FY2026 (201.5% YoY growth), full-year run rate ~$1.3B+
- Profitability: 68.6% gross margin, 31.8% net margin, 27.5% ROE
- Valuation: 21.7x forward P/E, virtually debt-free (D/E 0.88%)
- Growth: Q4 FY2026 guidance $425-435M, FY2027 guidance for >50% YoY growth

**Marvell (MRVL)**:
- Revenue: $8.19B FY2026 (42% YoY growth), targeting $15B by FY2028
- Custom silicon: $1.5B in FY2026, expected to double again by FY2028
- Profitability: 51% gross margin, 32.6% net margin, 19.3% ROE
- Valuation: 20.3x forward P/E, D/E 33.5%

## Bear Case

The most significant risk is NVIDIA vertical integration. If NVIDIA decides to design its own connectivity silicon in-house — as it has done with networking via the Mellanox acquisition — third-party suppliers like Credo and Marvell could see their TAM contract. NVIDIA's co-packaged optics initiative already suggests deeper involvement in the optical stack.

AI capex cyclicality is a structural concern. Both companies derive the majority of their growth from hyperscaler data center spending. If training efficiency gains (distillation, inference optimization) reduce compute requirements, or if hyperscalers pull back on capex due to ROI concerns, the interconnect buildout would slow proportionally. Credo's near-total dependence on AI infrastructure demand and customer concentration among a small number of hyperscalers amplifies this risk [4].

Broadcom's competitive scale cannot be ignored. As the dominant custom ASIC provider with deeper customer relationships and greater R&D resources, Broadcom could pressure both Credo and Marvell in custom silicon and optical DSP markets.

Finally, both stocks trade at elevated trailing P/E multiples (CRDO at 56x, MRVL at 36x), embedding significant growth expectations. Any deceleration from current trajectories would trigger multiple compression.

## Conclusion

The AI optical interconnect thesis rests on a physical constraint: electrical cables cannot scale beyond 800G, and every next-generation AI cluster requires 1.6T+ optical connectivity. NVIDIA's GTC 2026 confirmation of co-packaged optics in production systems validates the multi-year transition. Credo and Marvell are positioned as the two leading pure-play beneficiaries — Credo through AEC and optical hardware, Marvell through custom silicon and electro-optics DSPs. Together, they offer exposure to a $10B+ interconnect TAM growing at 30%+ annually, at forward P/E multiples of 20-22x against revenue growth of 40-200%. The key monitoring point is whether hyperscaler AI capex commitments translate into sustained interconnect orders through 2027-2028.
invalidation: NVIDIA announces in-house connectivity silicon that replaces third-party AEC or optical DSP suppliers, evidenced by a product roadmap that bypasses Credo or Marvell components
Hyperscaler AI capex declines more than 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters, signaling a structural slowdown in data center interconnect demand
Credo or Marvell revenue growth decelerates below 15% YoY for two consecutive quarters, indicating loss of competitive position or market saturation
